result:
**The Peculiar Art of Getting Lost**

There's something deeply unfashionable about getting lost these days. We carry satellites in our pockets, tiny mechanical prophets that whisper turn-by-turn instructions with the confidence of someone who has never once questioned their own existence. Yet I find myself increasingly drawn to the idea of deliberate disorientation, of choosing to not know where I am or where I'm going.

Last week, I turned off my phone and walked into a neighborhood I'd never explored. Within twenty minutes, I was thoroughly lost—not dangerously so, just pleasantly unmoored from my mental map of the city. The streets curved in unexpected ways, doubling back on themselves like a conversation with someone who keeps forgetting what they were trying to say. I passed a shop selling nothing but doorknobs, hundreds of them gleaming like bronze and brass promises. Who needs that many doorknobs? What doors are they opening? The mystery delighted me more than any answer could.

Getting lost is different from being lost. The latter implies distress, a problem requiring solution. The former is an activity, almost an art form. Children understand this instinctively. Watch a child in a new place—they don't move efficiently from point A to point B. They meander, investigate, double back, get distracted by patterns in the sidewalk or the way light falls through leaves. They are professional get-losters, and we spend years training this out of them, teaching them to value efficiency over exploration.

I think about the last time I was truly, memorably lost. Not geographically, but existentially—that peculiar feeling when life's normal patterns dissolve and you find yourself standing in the middle of your own existence without a clear sense of how you got there or where you're supposed to go next. It was after a relationship ended, one of those endings that feels both inevitable and impossible. I remember standing in my kitchen at 3 AM, looking at a tomato on my counter and having absolutely no idea why anyone had ever decided tomatoes were food. The familiar had become foreign. My own hands looked like they belonged to someone else.

That kind of lostness is terrifying, but also oddly liberating. When you don't know where you are, every direction is equally valid. When your internal compass spins wildly, you might as well follow your curiosity instead of your plans. During that period, I started taking pottery classes (terrible at it), learned to identify birds by their songs (still can't), and wrote long letters to friends I hadn't spoken to in years (half responded). None of it "helped" in any measurable way, but all of it mattered.

We've built a world that abhors disorientation. Every surface has a sign, every experience has a review, every question has been answered a thousand times on forums we can access instantly. We've GPS'd our way out of serendipity. But something vital happens in that space between knowing and not knowing, between here and there. It's where we discover things we didn't know we were looking for.

I once met a man who collected stories about getting lost. He had hundreds of them, recorded in neat handwriting in a series of notebooks. His favorite was about a businessman who got off at the wrong subway stop in Tokyo, too proud to admit his mistake, and spent an entire day pretending he meant to be in that random neighborhood. By the end of the day, he'd discovered a tiny restaurant that served the best ramen he'd ever tasted, met a woman who became his wife, and decided to change careers. "Getting lost," the collector told me, "is just another way of saying yes to the universe's suggestions."

There's a phrase in Portuguese—"desenrascanço"—which doesn't translate perfectly but roughly means the ability to disentangle yourself from a difficult situation through improvisation and resourcefulness. It's the art of finding your way when there is no way, of turning lostness into discovery. I think every culture should have such a word, a celebration of those moments when not knowing becomes a form of knowing.

Sometimes I wonder if we're raising a generation that will never truly get lost. They'll never take a wrong turn that leads to a perfect hidden bookstore, never misread a map and end up at a beach instead of a museum, never have to ask a stranger for directions and end up hearing their life story. These small disorientations are rehearsals for bigger uncertainties—career changes, relationship shifts, the inevitable losses that life brings. If we never practice being lost in small ways, how will we navigate being lost in large ones?

The doorknob shop, it turns out, also repairs old clocks. I discovered this when I went back to find it again, which took three tries because I'd been so thoroughly lost the first time. The owner told me that people bring him clocks that haven't worked in decades, hoping he can restore them. "Sometimes," he said, "the best thing is that they stay broken. A stopped clock is a memory of a specific moment. Why would you want to lose that?"

Perhaps that's what getting lost really offers us—a chance to stop time, to step outside the relentless forward march of progress and productivity. In lostness, we recover a different kind of time, circular and expansive, where discovery matters more than arrival, where the journey isn't a means but an end in itself.

Tomorrow, I think I'll get lost again. Deliberately, hopefully, with my phone turned off and my curiosity turned on. There's an entire city out there waiting to be not-found, streets that lead nowhere important except everywhere essential. And if I'm lucky, I'll discover something I didn't know I was looking for—perhaps just the reminder that being lost is sometimes the only way to find yourself.
